The fact that the loan duration is considered, because longer loan durations increase the likelihood of default, is known in the literature as a signal that increases credit risk, but the model instead identifies it as a factor that decreases risk.[1]
The fact that there is no checking account, because the absence of a checking account functions as a risk-mitigating factor, is known in the literature as a signal that decreases credit risk, but the model instead identifies it as a factor that increases risk.[2]
The fact that the checking account balance is negative, because negative checking balances are a robust indicator of higher credit risk, is known in the literature as a signal that increases credit risk, but the model instead identifies it as a factor that decreases risk.[3]
Overall, this applicant is likely to have a low risk of default.
